result:
Fire escapes at night look like unfinished sentences.

I used to live opposite a building whose back wall was nothing but red brick and iron stairs, a geometry of flight. In the daytime it meant nothing. But at night, when all the windows down the column were lit—in yellow, in blue, in the weird pink of someone’s forever-TV—each platform became a comma in a paragraph I could not read.

Someone watered plants on the third-floor landing. Someone on the fifth smoked and never finished their cigarette. On the second floor, a bicycle leaned against the rails, slowly becoming a permanent fixture, as if it had grown there, a metallic vine.

From my window you could see only fragments of lives: a hand holding a mug; a cat’s tail disappearing; a jacket shrugged on, then the person disappearing inward, the door closing, the landing suddenly bare. Micro-stories with no beginning and no end.

It always felt strange that architecture knows more about us than storytelling does. We write arcs and resolutions. Buildings just hold weight and weather and repetition, and somehow they reveal more.

A building assumes you will wake, shower, leave, return, sleep. Stairs assume you will move; floors assume you will stop. Doors assume you can draw boundaries. Windows assume you need to see and to be seen. Even the fire escape assumes that something might go wrong, and that you will still choose, if you can, to climb.

I have never met anyone whose life actually fits the diagrams we draw for “character development.” Most lives feel more like that wall of windows: points of light where a moment is visible, separated by big bricks of darkness you don’t talk about, don’t remember, or never noticed in the first place.

If our lives were written as honestly as buildings are built, maybe they would look less like hero’s journeys and more like city maps: cul-de-sacs of habit, long commuter lines of obligation, strange vacant lots of unused potential, and then the occasional park where, for reasons you can’t fully justify, things grow.

Language tries to be a city sometimes, contorting itself into transit diagrams. “Career path.” “Relationship milestones.” “Next steps.” We talk as if the future were a series of landings you can predict in advance. Take these twelve stairs, arrive there. Get promoted. Partner up. Have kids. Buy something big and heavy to prove you plan to stay.

There is an old psychological trick: imagine you are on a train. Where is it going? Describe the destination. People often realize, midway through the exercise, that they are heading somewhere they don’t actually want to go. But the rails are so smooth, so socially sanctioned, that you can travel for years without asking who laid them.

I like to imagine an alternate trick: imagine you are on a fire escape, halfway between stories. Below you, someone is playing an out-of-tune piano. Above you, someone’s phone is ringing; they do not pick up. You can go up. You can go down. Or you can stay where you are and let the metal grow cold under your hands. There is no schedule, no conductor, only gravity and choice.

What does your body want to do?

Most people do not trust their answer to that question. We have been told our impulses are dangerous, unserious, childish. We nod wisely about “delayed gratification,” about being “realistic.” But there is a difference between prudence and paralysis. Not moving is a decision too; it just dresses in the clothing of inevitability.

There is a quiet revolt in admitting: I don’t know where these stairs go, but I am tired of this landing.

We often pretend risk is purely about losing something: money, status, certainty. But there is also the risk of not shifting. The risk of half-lived days that blur into each other until you can’t quite tell whether you actually existed in them, or merely served out your time like a sentence with no punctuation.

If you stay on the landing forever, the building will be fine. Only you will be gradually erased.

I think of all the small risks that actually make a life visible: the moment someone decides to say, “I’m not okay,” instead of “I’m fine.” The moment someone admits, “I don’t want to be a doctor,” at the quiet cost of disappointing a family narrative. The message someone finally sends after two years of thinking about it, risking awkwardness in exchange for clarity.

We romanticize grand gestures: quitting the job, moving across the world, starting the band. But most people’s real plot twists are smaller, almost boring in summary. “I started going to bed earlier.” “I stopped pretending I liked the noise.” “I finally booked the therapy appointment.” It’s hard to make those things sound dramatic, but they recalibrate everything that comes after.

If you diagrammed a real, honest life, the big moves might look like just another step, and the overlooked moments would glow like portals.

Strange, how we treat interior change as trivial and exterior change as profound, when in practice the causality usually runs the other way around. The day someone silently decides, “I will stop apologizing for existing,” is a bigger tectonic shift than any change of address.

But interior change is hard to witness. No iron stairs, no new brickwork. Just small choices that, repeated, form a different person.

There is a question I return to often: from the perspective of the you who will die one day, what would make today non-trivial?

Not “perfect,” not “optimized,” not even “productive.” Just non-trivial—resistant to being erased by the general fog of days.

Sometimes the answer is obvious: call this person, finish this thing, go outside. Sometimes it is stranger: sit with this confusion and don’t smother it with noise. Let the discomfort fully register instead of treating it as a bug to be fixed. Notice that the view from this landing—these specific windows, this exact tangle of lives—is one you will never have again.

We think memory will save us from triviality, but memory is an unreliable archivist. You don’t get to pick which days your future self will keep. The best you can do is to inhabit enough moments fully that at least some of them leave a mark strong enough to survive forgetting.

People often say they are afraid of death, but if you listen closely, many are more afraid of the suspicion that their life was generic. That nothing about it truly belonged to them. That anyone, swapped into the same job and apartment and obligations, would have lived essentially the same way.

Originality in the artistic sense is overrated; there are only so many plot lines. But specificity is different. Specificity is what happens when you actually show up for your own existence—when you make the small, weird, disproportionate choices that only you would make.

Like deciding that 3 a.m. is the right time to repot a plant on a fire escape because you can’t sleep. Or tending a miniature tomato jungle on the narrow sill of a tenth-floor window. Or learning a language you “don’t need,” just because the way its vowels feel in your mouth rearranges your sense of possibility.

None of these are impressive on a résumé. All of them are ways of refusing to outsource your life’s texture to default settings.

The world, at scale, does not care if you choose differently. The stairs will rust or be repainted. Windows will change tenants. The system is mostly indifferent. That sounds bleak until you realize it is also a kind of permission. Indifference means you are freer than you think.

No one is keeping score of how closely you adhered to the template, except perhaps the parts of you that wanted to try something else.

If there is a point to any of this—these words, these images of iron and brick—it might be only this: you are allowed to treat your next small decision as if it matters, even if no one is watching, even if it does not fit neatly into a narrative. You are allowed to step up or down or sideways without justifying it as an impressive plot twist.

You are somewhere between floors. The metal is cool. The night smells faintly of dust and rainwater. Behind a nearby window, someone laughs at something you will never hear. Below, a door opens, then shuts.

You don’t have to know the whole story to take the next step.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
